title: Chamomile infusion for mild colic
subtitle: A gentle antispasmodic for post-meal cramping
excerpt: A hot infusion of dried chamomile flowers, taken after a meal for mild cramping or colicky discomfort in adults. One of the most widely documented western herbal preparations for digestive spasm.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), chamomile entry, antispasmodic and carminative actions. EMA HMPC monograph on Matricaria flos (2015), modern conservative evidence summary for digestive use. WHO Monograph Volume 1 (1999), cross-tradition documentation.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried chamomile flowers keep 12 months in a sealed tin away from light. Brew fresh for each cup.

recipeTools.notes: Any teapot with a lid. The lid is essential.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb traditionally associated with easing smooth-muscle spasm in the gut, uterus, or respiratory tract.

content.text: A cup of chamomile after a meal that has left the stomach cramping. Chamomile's

action on the gut has been documented since Culpeper (1652) and is confirmed in the EMA's 2015 monograph, the herb relaxes smooth muscle in the digestive tract and eases the cramping pattern that follows a gassy, over-large, or poorly digested meal. The preparation is the same as for chamomile's nervine use: two teaspoons of dried flowers in a lidded pot, hot water just off the boil, steeped covered for ten minutes.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Allergen: chamomile is in the Asteraceae (daisy) family. Readers with a known allergy to ragweed, chrysanthemum, calendula, or related plants should patch-test before internal use, dab a small amount of cooled infusion on the forearm and wait 24 hours.

Pregnancy: a single culinary-strength cup of chamomile is widely tolerated. Therapeutic-dose chamomile (multiple strong cups daily) is best discussed with a midwife rather than self-prescribed in pregnancy.

Children: for school-age children, half-strength chamomile (1 teaspoon per cup) is within the traditional range. For infants and toddlers, consult a paediatric herbalist or GP, the old continental tradition of chamomile gripe water for infant colic is herbalist-supervised, not a home self-care recommendation.

See your doctor for severe or worsening abdominal pain, colic that comes with fever, colic that does not ease within a few hours, or any colic in a child under two.

items.prepNote: about 2 heaped teaspoons of loose-leaf dried chamomile flowers

content.text: Place {{dried-chamomile-flowers}} of dried chamomile flowers in the teapot or infuser. Boil the kettle, wait 30 seconds, then pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over the flowers. Put the lid on the pot or a saucer over the cup immediately. Steep for 10 minutes, the lid keeps the apple-and-honey volatile oils in the cup rather than letting them escape as steam. Strain through the infuser or a fine sieve. Sip slowly after the meal, while still warm.

One cup after the meal that caused the cramping. Up to three cups a day during an episode, after each main meal, for adults. The traditional course is two to three days at most; if cramping continues beyond that, the cause needs investigation. Chamomile at culinary-tea strength every day long-term is generally safe, but daily cramping is a symptom the GP should assess.

Dried chamomile flowers keep 12 months in a sealed tin or dark glass jar away from light and heat. Flowers that have lost their apple-honey scent when the tin is opened have lost much of their active volatile oil, refresh the stock. Brewed chamomile tea does not keep; make each cup fresh.
When NOT to use this remedy

See your doctor for severe or worsening abdominal pain, pain with fever, pain with vomiting or diarrhoea, or any abdominal pain in a child under two. See your GP for colic that recurs daily for more than a week or comes with weight loss. Skip chamomile if you have a known Asteraceae allergy.
